text: The surgeon continues dissection of the small bowel, freeing it from the colon. The retrocholic alimentary limb is visualized and maneuvered from the inframesocolic area to the supracolonic area.

The surgeon carefully dissects the dilated stomach, separating the omentum from the suture line of the sleeve gastrectomy. The mesenterium is divided using a vessel sealer, and the small bowel is divided with an endogea tan load. Posterior attachments of the left gastrectomy are detached, with the left gastric artery identified and preserved, while the right gastric artery is taken down.

The surgeon creates a five-centimeter gastric pouch using an Endogea black load 60 stapler. A 40 French Blanc bougie is inserted into the gastric pouch to guide its formation. The remnant distal stomach and duodenal jejunostomy are placed into an endopouch and extracted through a 15-millimeter trocar.

The surgeon dissects the hiatus, opens the gastroesophageal membrane, and accesses the mediastinum. A retroesophageal window is created, allowing the esophagus to be completely dissected into the mediastinum. The esophagus is mobilized so that three centimeters can be brought back into the abdomen without tension. The hiatal hernia is repaired using two interrupted 0-Ethibond sutures.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm of the robotic system to elevate the alimentary limb into the upper abdomen. A new gastrogenostomy is created, with the anastomosis constructed in two layers. The posterior layer is sutured using a running three-zero PDS V-lock, while the inner and external layers are completed with running three-zero PDS sutures.

The surgeon clamps the alimentary limb and performs an intraoperative endoscopy, revealing a well-formed anastomosis and a small gastric pouch with no leaks. The omentum is reattached to the lateral suture line of the pouch using 3-0 PDS V-lock sutures to release tension and stabilize the pouch. There is no visible blood loss, and no drains are placed.
